5 min read
Just now

--

If you are building custom activities for UiPath, there is one concept that most tutorials never explain clearly — and it can cost you hours of debugging when you get it wrong.
This article explains how UiPath identifies activities internally, why namespace and package name changes break existing workflows, and how to recover quickly when this happens.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How UiPath Identifies a Custom Activity
When you drag a custom activity into a UiPath workflow, Studio does not store the activity by its display name or package version. It stores it by its Fully Qualified Type Name:
Namespace + ClassName + Assembly NameOpen any UiPath workflow .xaml file in a text editor and you will see something like this at the top:
xmlns:abc="clr-namespace:abc;assembly=Custom_Activities"And inside the workflow body:
<abc:MyActivity OperandA="10" OperandB="5" />This is the permanent identity record. Every time Studio loads that workflow, it uses this information to locate and load the correct activity from the installed package.
This identity is written at design time — the moment you first use the activity. It does not update automatically when you update the package.

What NuGet Actually Does During a Package Update
This is the part most developers assume incorrectly.
When you update a NuGet package in Studio, NuGet replaces the DLL files inside your project’s packages folder. That is all it does.
It does not scan your workflow files. It does not update namespace references. It does not touch XAML in any way.
So the workflow XAML always holds the identity from when the activity was first used — not from the latest installed version.
Package Update → DLL replaced ✅
Package Update → XAML updated ❌ (never happens)This distinction is critical.

Why a Namespace Change Breaks Existing Workflows
Consider this scenario:
Version Namespace Package Name 1.0.5 CompanyName.Activities Custom_Activities 1.0.9 CompanyName.Automation Custom_Activities
A workflow built using version 1.0.5 has this in its XAML:
xmlns:ca="clr-namespace:CompanyName.Activities;assembly=Custom_Activities"After updating to version 1.0.9, the DLL now contains CompanyName.Automation namespace. But the XAML still references CompanyName.Activities.
Studio looks for CompanyName.Activities.MyActivity inside the DLL. It does not exist anymore. The activity shows an error. The workflow is broken.
The namespace changed. The identity changed. The XAML was never updated. Mismatch.

Why a Package Name Change Is Even More Disruptive
Renaming your NuGet package is treated by NuGet as creating an entirely new package — not as an update to the existing one.
Custom_Activities → Custom_Activitie (different package entirely)From NuGet’s perspective, Custom_Activities and Custom_Activitie have no relationship. Consumers of Custom_Activities will never receive updates from Custom_Activitie. They are orphaned on the old package permanently — unless they manually uninstall the old one and install the new one.
This is a common mistake when package names are finalized quickly during early development. One typo, one naming decision changed later — and all existing consumers are disconnected from future updates.

The Recovery — Direct XAML Editing
When a namespace mismatch breaks a workflow, the fastest fix is not reinstalling the package or replacing activities manually. It is editing the XAML directly.
Open the affected .xaml file in VS Code or Notepad++ and use Find and Replace:
Find: clr-namespace:OldNamespace
Replace: clr-namespace:NewNamespaceThen:
Find: <OldNamespace:
Replace: <NewNamespace:And:
Find: </OldNamespace:
Replace: </NewNamespace:For projects with multiple .xaml files, use Find in Files (Ctrl + Shift + F in Notepad++) to apply the replacement across the entire project folder at once.
Always take a backup before editing XAML directly. XAML is XML — a single malformed tag can make the entire workflow unreadable in Studio.

The Broader Engineering Principle
This is not a UiPath-specific quirk. It is a fundamental software engineering concept.
In software, there is a clear separation between two things:
Implementation — the internal logic of your code. This can change freely across versions. Bug fixes, performance improvements, refactoring, new features. Consumers benefit from these changes transparently.
Contract — the public identity that consumers depend on. This must never change once established in production. Changing it is a breaking change regardless of how small it seems.
In UiPath custom activities, the contract is:
Namespace + ClassName + Package NameThe same principle applies across the industry. In REST API design, changing an endpoint path breaks every client using that path. In Python package development, renaming a module breaks every import statement. In LangChain tool development, renaming a tool breaks every agent that references it by name.
The pattern is consistent. Public contracts are permanent. Internal implementations are flexible.

Best Practices for Custom Activity Development
Finalize namespace and package name before the first production deployment. Once any workflow consumes your activity, the namespace and package name are frozen. There is no safe way to change them without a migration plan for all consumers.
Use a consistent naming convention from day one. A structure like CompanyName.Domain.Activities is clear, scalable, and unlikely to need renaming later. Avoid generic names that might need clarification over time.
Never rename a production NuGet package. If you absolutely must rename — keep the old package alive with a deprecation notice and provide the new package separately. Give consumers time to migrate.
Treat internal logic as flexible, public identity as permanent. Add new activities to the package freely. Refactor internal classes. Improve algorithms. None of this affects existing workflows. Only namespace, class name, and package name changes affect identity.
When XAML breaks — edit XAML directly. Manual activity replacement is slow and error-prone for large workflows. Direct XAML editing with Find and Replace is faster, more reliable, and works across multiple files simultaneously.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Summary
UiPath identifies every custom activity by its Fully Qualified Type Name — Namespace + ClassName + Assembly Name. This identity is written into workflow XAML at design time and never updated automatically by NuGet.
Changing namespace or package name creates a new identity. Every workflow referencing the old identity breaks — regardless of whether the change was later reverted.
The fix for a namespace mismatch is direct XAML editing, not package reinstallation.
The prevention is treating namespace and package name as permanent public contracts from the moment they reach production.
Understanding this distinction — between versioning your implementation and freezing your identity — is what separates stable, maintainable custom activity libraries from ones that regularly break workflows during updates.
